infobox.value: * Signing of the Treaty of Versailles (1919) * Crowds gathering outside the [New York Stock Exchange](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange") (1929) * Japanese troops in Shanghai (1937) * [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") in [Brno](/wiki/Brno "Brno") (1939) | |

lasted from 11 November 1918 to 1 September 1939 (20 years, 9 months, 21 days) – from the end of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") (WWI) to the beginning of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") (WWII). It was relatively short, yet featured many social, political, military, and economic changes throughout the world. Petroleum-based energy production and associated mechanisation led to the prosperous [Roaring Twenties](/wiki/Roaring_Twenties "Roaring Twenties"), a time of [social](/wiki/Social_mobility "Social mobility") and [economic mobility](/wiki/Economic_mobility "Economic mobility") for the [middle class](/wiki/Middle_class "Middle class"). [Automobiles](/wiki/Automobile "Automobile"), [electric lighting](/wiki/Electric_light "Electric light"), [radio](/wiki/Radio "Radio"), and more became common among populations in the [first world](/wiki/Developed_world "Developed world"). The era's indulgences were followed by the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), an unprecedented worldwide economic downturn that severely damaged many of the world's largest economies.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/BlankMap-World-1921.png/330px-BlankMap-World-1921.png)](/wiki/File:BlankMap-World-1921.png)

Global borders in 1920

In the [history of the 20th century](/wiki/History_of_the_20th_century "History of the 20th century"), the **interwar period** lasted from 11 November 1918 to 1 September 1939 (20 years, 9 months, 21 days) – from the end of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") (WWI) to the beginning of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") (WWII). It was relatively short, yet featured many social, political, military, and economic changes throughout the world. Petroleum-based energy production and associated mechanisation led to the prosperous [Roaring Twenties](/wiki/Roaring_Twenties "Roaring Twenties"), a time of [social](/wiki/Social_mobility "Social mobility") and [economic mobility](/wiki/Economic_mobility "Economic mobility") for the [middle class](/wiki/Middle_class "Middle class"). [Automobiles](/wiki/Automobile "Automobile"), [electric lighting](/wiki/Electric_light "Electric light"), [radio](/wiki/Radio "Radio"), and more became common among populations in the [first world](/wiki/Developed_world "Developed world"). The era's indulgences were followed by the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), an unprecedented worldwide economic downturn that severely damaged many of the world's largest economies.

Politically, the era coincided with the rise of [communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism"), starting in Russia with the [October Revolution](/wiki/October_Revolution "October Revolution") and [Russian Civil War](/wiki/Russian_Civil_War "Russian Civil War"), at the end of WWI, and ended with the rise of [fascism](/wiki/Fascism "Fascism"), particularly in Germany and Italy. China was in the midst of a half-century of instability and the [Chinese Civil War](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War") between the [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang"), the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party"), and many warlords. The empires of [Britain](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), [France](/wiki/French_colonial_empire "French colonial empire"), and others faced challenges as [imperialism](/wiki/Imperialism "Imperialism") was increasingly viewed negatively and independence movements emerged in many [colonies](/wiki/Colony "Colony"); in Europe, after protracted low-level fighting [most of Ireland became independent](/wiki/Irish_War_of_Independence "Irish War of Independence").

The [Russian](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), [Austro-Hungarian](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary"), and [German Empires](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") were dismantled, with the [Ottoman territories](/wiki/Partition_of_the_Ottoman_Empire "Partition of the Ottoman Empire") and [German colonies](/wiki/German_colonial_empire "German colonial empire") redistributed among the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I"), chiefly Britain and France. In Russia, the [Bolsheviks](/wiki/Bolsheviks "Bolsheviks") managed to regain control of [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus "Belarus") and [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"), [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia"), and the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus"), forming the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"). The western parts of the Russian Empire, [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"), [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia"), [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania"), and [Poland](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic") became independent nations in their own right, and [Bessarabia](/wiki/Bessarabia "Bessarabia") (now [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova "Moldova") and parts of [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine")) [chose to reunify](/wiki/Union_of_Bessarabia_with_Romania "Union of Bessarabia with Romania") with [Romania](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Romania "Kingdom of Romania"). In the [Near East](/wiki/Near_East "Near East"), [Egypt](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Egypt "Kingdom of Egypt") and [Iraq](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Iraq "Kingdom of Iraq") gained independence, while [Nejd](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Nejd "Sultanate of Nejd") conquered [Hejaz](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hejaz "Kingdom of Hejaz"), eventually becoming the [Kingdom of Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

During the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), countries in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") nationalised many foreign companies (most of which belonged to the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States")) in a bid to strengthen their own economies. The territorial ambitions of the Japanese, Italians, and Germans led to the expansion of their domains.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The Soviets, similarly, harbored territorial ambitions during this period and sought to revise the post-WWI settlements.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Militarily, there was a markedly rapid advance in technology in the period, which, alongside lessons-learned from WWI, brought about strategic and tactical innovations.[[4]](#cite_note-4) While the period would largely see a continuation of the development of the technologies pioneered in WWI, debates emerged as to the most effective use of these advancements.[[5]](#cite_note-5) On land, discussions focused on how [armoured](/wiki/Armoured_warfare "Armoured warfare"), [mechanised](/wiki/Mechanized_infantry "Mechanized infantry"), and [motorised](/wiki/Motorized_infantry "Motorized infantry") forces should be employed, particularly in-relation to the traditional branches of the [regular infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry"), [horse cavalry](/wiki/Horse_cavalry "Horse cavalry"), and [artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery").[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) In the air, the question of allocating [air forces](/wiki/Air_forces "Air forces") to [strategic bombing](/wiki/Strategic_bombing "Strategic bombing") versus dedicating such forces to frontline [close air support](/wiki/Close_air_support "Close air support") was the primary contention, with some arguing that [interceptor](/wiki/Interceptor_aircraft "Interceptor aircraft") development was outpacing [bombers](/wiki/Bombers "Bombers"), and others maintaining that "[the bomber will always get through](/wiki/The_bomber_will_always_get_through "The bomber will always get through")." In the [naval](/wiki/Navy "Navy") sphere, the primary question was whether [battleships](/wiki/Battleships "Battleships") would maintain their dominance of the seas or be rendered virtually obsolete by [naval aviation](/wiki/Naval_aviation "Naval aviation").[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The interwar period ended on 1 September 1939 with the [German](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") [invasion of Poland](/wiki/Invasion_of_Poland "Invasion of Poland") and the start of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[10]](#cite_note-Overy-2016-10)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Via the events of WWII, the military deliberations and controversies characteristic of the interwar period ultimately were resolved,[[11]](#cite_note-11) which served as a foundation for many of the tenets, [doctrines](/wiki/Military_doctrine "Military doctrine"), and strategies of [modern warfare](/wiki/Modern_warfare "Modern warfare").[[12]](#cite_note-12) Overall, the innovations of WWI and the interwar period would see a shift away from traditional [line-](/wiki/Line_of_battle "Line of battle") and [front-based warfare](/wiki/Front_line "Front line") and towards a significantly more mobile, mechanised, and [asymmetric](/wiki/Asymmetric_warfare "Asymmetric warfare") form of combat.

## Turmoil in Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interwar_period&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Turmoil in Europe")]

Main article: [Aftermath of World War I](/wiki/Aftermath_of_World_War_I "Aftermath of World War I")

Further information: [European interwar economy](/wiki/European_interwar_economy "European interwar economy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Mapa_da_Europa_em_01011919.png/250px-Mapa_da_Europa_em_01011919.png)](/wiki/File:Mapa_da_Europa_em_01011919.png)

Map of Europe on January 1, 1919

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Europe_in_1923.jpg/500px-Europe_in_1923.jpg)](/wiki/File:Europe_in_1923.jpg)

Map of Europe in 1923

After the [Armistice of Compiègne](/wiki/Armistice_of_11_November_1918 "Armistice of 11 November 1918") on 11 November 1918, which ended the fighting of World War I, the years 1918–1924 were marked by turmoil as the [Russian Civil War](/wiki/Russian_Civil_War "Russian Civil War") continued to rage on, and [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") struggled to recover from the devastation of the First World War and the destabilising effects of not just the collapse of the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), but the destruction of the [German](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire"), [Austro-Hungarian](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary"), and [Ottoman Empires](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), as well. There were numerous new or restored countries in Southern, Central, and Eastern Europe, some small in size including [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania") and [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia"), and some larger, such as [Poland](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic") and the [Kingdom of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia"). The United States gained dominance in world finance. Thus, when Germany refused to pay further war reparations to Britain, France and other former members of the [Entente](/wiki/Entente_Cordiale "Entente Cordiale"), the Americans came up with the [Dawes Plan](/wiki/Dawes_Plan "Dawes Plan") and Wall Street invested heavily in Germany, which repaid its reparations to nations that, in turn, used the dollars to pay off their war debts to Washington. By the middle of the decade, prosperity was widespread, with the second half of the decade known as the [Roaring Twenties](/wiki/Roaring_Twenties "Roaring Twenties").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## International relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interwar_period&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: International relations")]

Main article: [International relations (1919–1939)](/wiki/International_relations_(1919%E2%80%931939) "International relations (1919–1939)")

The important stages of interwar diplomacy and international relations included resolutions of wartime issues, such as reparations owed by Germany and boundaries; American involvement in European finances and disarmament projects; the expectations and failures of the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations");[[14]](#cite_note-14) the relationships of the new countries to the old; the distrustful relations of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") to the capitalist world; peace and disarmament efforts; responses to the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") starting in 1929; the collapse of world trade; the collapse of democratic regimes one by one; the growth of efforts at economic [autarky](/wiki/Autarky "Autarky"); Japanese aggressiveness toward [China](/wiki/Republic_of_China "Republic of China"), occupying large amounts of Chinese land, as well as [border disputes between the Soviet Union and Japan](/wiki/Soviet%E2%80%93Japanese_border_conflicts "Soviet–Japanese border conflicts"), leading to multiple clashes along the Soviet and Japanese occupied Manchurian border; fascist diplomacy, including the aggressive moves by Mussolini's Italy and Hitler's Germany; the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War"); Italy's [invasion and occupation of Abyssinia (Ethiopia)](/wiki/Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War "Second Italo-Ethiopian War") in the [Horn of Africa](/wiki/Horn_of_Africa "Horn of Africa"); the [appeasement](/wiki/Appeasement "Appeasement") of Germany's expansionist moves against the German-speaking nation of [Austria](/wiki/Federal_State_of_Austria "Federal State of Austria"), the region inhabited by ethnic Germans called the [Sudetenland](/wiki/Sudetenland "Sudetenland") in [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia"), the [remilitarisation of the League of Nations demilitarised zone of the German Rhineland](/wiki/Remilitarization_of_the_Rhineland "Remilitarization of the Rhineland") region, and the last, desperate stages of rearmament as the Second World War increasingly loomed.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Disarmament was a very popular public policy. However, the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") played little role in this effort, with the United States and Britain taking the lead. U.S. Secretary of State [Charles Evans Hughes](/wiki/Charles_Evans_Hughes "Charles Evans Hughes") sponsored the [Washington Naval Conference](/wiki/Washington_Naval_Conference "Washington Naval Conference") of 1921 in determining how many capital ships each major country was allowed. The new allocations were actually followed and there were no naval races in the 1920s. Britain played a leading role in the 1927 [Geneva Naval Conference](/wiki/Geneva_Naval_Conference "Geneva Naval Conference") and the 1930 London Conference that led to the [London Naval Treaty](/wiki/London_Naval_Treaty "London Naval Treaty"), which added cruisers and submarines to the list of ship allocations. However the refusal of Japan, Germany, Italy and the USSR to go along with this led to the meaningless [Second London Naval Treaty](/wiki/Second_London_Naval_Treaty "Second London Naval Treaty") of 1936. Naval disarmament had collapsed and the issue became rearming for a war against Germany and Japan.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Roaring Twenties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interwar_period&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Roaring Twenties")]

Main articles: [1920s](/wiki/1920s "1920s"), [Roaring Twenties](/wiki/Roaring_Twenties "Roaring Twenties"), [Golden Twenties](/wiki/Golden_Twenties "Golden Twenties"), and [Années folles](/wiki/Ann%C3%A9es_folles "Années folles")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Douglas_Fairbanks_and_Mary_Pickford_02.jpg/250px-Douglas_Fairbanks_and_Mary_Pickford_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Douglas_Fairbanks_and_Mary_Pickford_02.jpg)

Actors [Douglas Fairbanks](/wiki/Douglas_Fairbanks "Douglas Fairbanks") and [Mary Pickford](/wiki/Mary_Pickford "Mary Pickford") in 1920

The [Roaring Twenties](/wiki/Roaring_Twenties "Roaring Twenties") highlighted novel and highly visible social and cultural trends and innovations. These trends, made possible by sustained economic prosperity, were most visible in major cities including [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), and [London](/wiki/London "London"). The [Jazz Age](/wiki/Jazz_Age "Jazz Age") began and [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco") peaked.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) For women, knee-length skirts and dresses became socially acceptable, as did bobbed hair with a [Marcel wave](/wiki/Marcelling "Marcelling"). The young women who pioneered these trends were called "[flappers](/wiki/Flapper "Flapper")".[[20]](#cite_note-20) Not all was new: ["normalcy" returned](/wiki/Return_to_normalcy "Return to normalcy") to politics in the wake of hyper-emotional wartime passions in the United States, France, and Germany.[[21]](#cite_note-21) The leftist revolutions in Finland, Poland, Germany, Austria, Hungary, and Spain were defeated by conservatives, but succeeded in Russia, which became the base for [Soviet communism](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "Communist Party of the Soviet Union") and [Marxism–Leninism](/wiki/Marxism%E2%80%93Leninism "Marxism–Leninism").[[22]](#cite_note-22) In Italy, the [National Fascist Party](/wiki/National_Fascist_Party "National Fascist Party") came to power under [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") after threatening a [March on Rome](/wiki/March_on_Rome "March on Rome") in 1922.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Most independent countries enacted [women's suffrage](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage "Women's suffrage") in the interwar era, including [Canada](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage_in_Canada "Women's suffrage in Canada") in 1917 (though [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec") held out longer), [Britain](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage_in_the_United_Kingdom "Women's suffrage in the United Kingdom") in 1918, and the [United States](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage_in_the_United_States "Women's suffrage in the United States") in 1920. There were a few major countries that held out until after the Second World War (such as France, Switzerland, and Portugal).[[24]](#cite_note-24) [Leslie Hume](/wiki/Leslie_Hume "Leslie Hume") said,

> The women's contribution to the war effort combined with failures of the previous systems' of Government made it more difficult than hitherto to maintain that women were, both by constitution and temperament, unfit to vote. If women could work in munitions factories, it seemed both ungrateful and illogical to deny them a place in the polling booth. But the vote was much more than simply a reward for war work; the point was that women's participation in the war helped to dispel the fears that surrounded women's entry into the public arena.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In Europe, according to Derek Aldcroft and Steven Morewood, "Nearly all countries registered some economic progress in the 1920s and most of them managed to regain or surpass their pre-war income and production levels by the end of the decade." The Netherlands, Norway, Sweden, Switzerland, and Greece did especially well, while Eastern Europe did poorly, due to the First World War and [Russian Civil War](/wiki/Russian_Civil_War "Russian Civil War").[[26]](#cite_note-26) In advanced economies the prosperity reached [middle class](/wiki/Middle_class "Middle class") households and many in the [working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class") with [radio](/wiki/Radio "Radio"), [automobiles](/wiki/Car "Car"), [telephones](/wiki/Telephone "Telephone"), and [electric lighting](/wiki/Electric_light "Electric light") and [appliances](/wiki/Home_appliance "Home appliance"). There was unprecedented industrial growth, accelerated consumer demand and aspirations, and significant changes in lifestyle and culture. The media began to focus on celebrities, especially sports heroes and movie stars. Major cities built large sports stadiums for the fans, in addition to palatial cinemas. The [mechanisation of agriculture](/wiki/Mechanised_agriculture "Mechanised agriculture") continued apace, producing an expansion of output, which lowered prices, and made many farm workers redundant. Often they moved to nearby industrial towns and cities.

## Great Depression

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interwar_period&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Great Depression")]

Main article: [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Unemployed_men_queued_outside_a_depression_soup_kitchen_opened_in_Chicago_by_Al_Capone%2C_02-1931_-_NARA_-_541927.jpg/250px-Unemployed_men_queued_outside_a_depression_soup_kitchen_opened_in_Chicago_by_Al_Capone%2C_02-1931_-_NARA_-_541927.jpg)](/wiki/File:Unemployed_men_queued_outside_a_depression_soup_kitchen_opened_in_Chicago_by_Al_Capone,_02-1931_-_NARA_-_541927.jpg)

Unemployed men outside a [soup kitchen](/wiki/Soup_kitchen "Soup kitchen") opened by Chicago gangster [Al Capone](/wiki/Al_Capone "Al Capone") during the [Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression_in_the_United_States "Great Depression in the United States"), 1931

The [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") was a severe worldwide [economic depression](/wiki/Depression_(economics) "Depression (economics)") that took place after 1929. The timing varied across nations; in most countries it started in 1929 and lasted until the late 1930s.[[27]](#cite_note-Garraty_Great_Depression-27) It was the longest, deepest, and most widespread depression of the 20th century.[[28]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-28) The depression originated in the United States and became worldwide news with the [stock market crash](/wiki/Stock_market_crash "Stock market crash") of 29 October 1929 (known as [Black Tuesday](/wiki/Wall_Street_crash_of_1929 "Wall Street crash of 1929")). Between 1929 and 1932, worldwide GDP fell by an estimated 15%. By comparison, worldwide GDP fell by less than 1% from 2008 to 2009 during the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession").[[29]](#cite_note-29) Some economies started to recover by the mid-1930s. However, in many countries, the negative effects of the Great Depression lasted until the beginning of World War II.[[27]](#cite_note-Garraty_Great_Depression-27): ch 1

The Great Depression had devastating effects in countries both [rich](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country") and [poor](/wiki/Developing_country "Developing country"). [Personal income](/wiki/Personal_income "Personal income"), [tax revenue](/wiki/Tax_revenue "Tax revenue"), [profits](/wiki/Profit_(economics) "Profit (economics)"), and [prices](/wiki/Price "Price") dropped, while [international trade](/wiki/International_trade "International trade") plunged by more than 50%. [Unemployment in the United States](/wiki/Unemployment_in_the_United_States "Unemployment in the United States") rose to 25% and in some countries rose as high as 33%.[[30]](#cite_note-Frank_Bernanke-30) Prices fell sharply, especially for mining and agricultural commodities. Business profits fell sharply as well, with a sharp reduction in new business starts.

[Cities all around the world](/wiki/Cities_in_the_Great_Depression "Cities in the Great Depression") were hit hard, especially those dependent on [heavy industry](/wiki/Heavy_industry "Heavy industry"). Construction was virtually halted in many countries. Farming communities and rural areas suffered as crop prices fell by about 60%.[[31]](#cite_note-USBLS-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33) Facing plummeting demand with few alternative sources of jobs, areas dependent on [primary sector industries](/wiki/Primary_sector_of_the_economy "Primary sector of the economy") such as mining and logging suffered the most.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

The [Weimar Republic](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic") in Germany gave way to two episodes of political and economic turmoil, the first culminated in the [German hyperinflation of 1923](/wiki/Hyperinflation_in_the_Weimar_Republic "Hyperinflation in the Weimar Republic") and the failed [Beer Hall Putsch](/wiki/Beer_Hall_Putsch "Beer Hall Putsch") of the same year. The second convulsion, brought on by the worldwide depression and [Germany's disastrous monetary policies](/wiki/Great_Depression_in_Germany#Government_responses "Great Depression in Germany"), resulted in the further rise of [Nazism](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany").[[35]](#cite_note-35) In Asia, [Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") became an ever more assertive power, especially with regard to [China](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%931949) "Republic of China (1912–1949)").[[36]](#cite_note-36)

### The rise of fascism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interwar_period&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: The rise of fascism")]

Main articles: [Fascism](/wiki/Fascism "Fascism") and [European interwar dictatorships](/wiki/European_interwar_dictatorships "European interwar dictatorships")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-H12943%2C_M%C3%BCnchener_Abkommen%2C_Hitler_und_Mussolini.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-H12943%2C_M%C3%BCnchener_Abkommen%2C_Hitler_und_Mussolini.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-H12943,_M%C3%BCnchener_Abkommen,_Hitler_und_Mussolini.jpg)

Cheering crowds greet [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") and [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") in [Munich, Germany](/wiki/Munich "Munich"), 1938

Democracy and prosperity largely went together in the 1920s. Economic disaster led to a distrust in the effectiveness of democracy and its collapse in much of Europe and Latin America, including the Baltic and Balkan countries, Poland, Spain, and Portugal. Powerful expansionary anti-democratic regimes emerged in Italy, Japan, and Germany.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

[Fascism](/wiki/Italian_fascism "Italian fascism") took control of the [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") in 1922; as the Great Depression worsened, [Nazism](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism") emerged victorious in Germany, fascism spread to many other countries in Europe, and also played a major role in several countries in Latin America.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Fascist parties sprang up, attuned to local right-wing traditions, but also possessing common features that typically included extreme militaristic nationalism, a desire for economic self-containment, threats and aggression toward neighbouring countries, oppression of minorities, a ridicule of democracy while using its techniques to mobilise an angry middle-class base, and a disgust with [cultural liberalism](/wiki/Cultural_liberalism "Cultural liberalism"). Fascists believed in power, violence, male superiority, and a "natural" hierarchy, often led by dictators such as [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") or [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler"). Fascism in power meant that liberalism and human rights were discarded, and individual pursuits and values were subordinated to what the party decided was best.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

## Empire of Japan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interwar_period&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Empire of Japan")]

Main articles: [Empire of Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan"), [Japanese colonial empire](/wiki/Japanese_colonial_empire "Japanese colonial empire"), [List of territories occupied by Imperial Japan](/wiki/List_of_territories_occupied_by_Imperial_Japan "List of territories occupied by Imperial Japan"), and [Statism in Shōwa Japan](/wiki/Statism_in_Sh%C5%8Dwa_Japan "Statism in Shōwa Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Pacific_Area_-_The_Imperial_Powers_1939_-_Map.svg/250px-Pacific_Area_-_The_Imperial_Powers_1939_-_Map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Pacific_Area_-_The_Imperial_Powers_1939_-_Map.svg)

Political map of the Asia-Pacific region, 1939

The Japanese modelled their [industrial economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Japan "Economy of Japan") closely on the most advanced Western European models. They began with textiles, railways, and shipping, expanding to electricity and machinery. The most serious weakness was a shortage of raw materials. Industry ran short of copper, and coal became a net importer. A deep flaw in the aggressive military strategy was a heavy dependence on imports including 100 per cent of the aluminium, 85 per cent of the iron ore, and especially 79 per cent of the oil supplies. It was one thing to go to war with China or Russia, but quite another to be in conflict with the key suppliers, especially the United States, Britain, and the Netherlands, of oil and iron.[[40]](#cite_note-FairbankReischauerCraig-40)

Japan joined the [Allies of the First World War](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I") to make territorial gains. Together with the British Empire, it divided up Germany's territories scattered in the Pacific and on the [Chinese coast](/wiki/Coastline_of_China "Coastline of China"); they did not amount to very much. The other Allies pushed back hard against Japan's efforts to dominate China through the [Twenty-One Demands](/wiki/Twenty-One_Demands "Twenty-One Demands") of 1915. Its [occupation of Siberia](/wiki/Japanese_intervention_in_Siberia "Japanese intervention in Siberia") proved unproductive. Japan's wartime diplomacy and limited military action had produced few results, and at the Paris Versailles peace conference at the end of the war, Japan was frustrated in its ambitions. At the [Paris Peace Conference](/wiki/Paris_Peace_Conference_(1919%E2%80%931920) "Paris Peace Conference (1919–1920)") in 1919, its [Racial Equality Proposal](/wiki/Racial_Equality_Proposal "Racial Equality Proposal") led to increasing diplomatic isolation. The 1902 alliance with Britain was not renewed in 1922 because of heavy pressure on Britain from Canada and the United States. In the 1920s Japanese diplomacy was rooted in a largely liberal democratic political system, and favoured internationalism. By 1930, however, Japan was rapidly reversing itself, rejecting democracy at home, as the Army seized more and more power, and rejecting internationalism and liberalism. By the late 1930s it had joined the Axis military alliance with Nazi Germany and Fascist Italy.[[40]](#cite_note-FairbankReischauerCraig-40): 563–612, 666

In 1930, the London disarmament conference angered the [Imperial Japanese Armed Forces](/wiki/Imperial_Japanese_Armed_Forces "Imperial Japanese Armed Forces"). The [Imperial Japanese Navy](/wiki/Imperial_Japanese_Navy "Imperial Japanese Navy") demanded parity with the United States, Britain and France, but was rejected and the conference kept the 1921 ratios. Japan was required to scrap a [capital ship](/wiki/Capital_ship "Capital ship"). Extremists assassinated Japanese Prime Minister [Inukai Tsuyoshi](/wiki/Inukai_Tsuyoshi "Inukai Tsuyoshi") in the [May 15 Incident](/wiki/May_15_Incident "May 15 Incident") and the military took more power, leading to rapid [democratic backsliding](/wiki/Democratic_backsliding "Democratic backsliding").[[41]](#cite_note-41)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Zhang_Xueliang_and_Chiang_Kai-shek.jpg/250px-Zhang_Xueliang_and_Chiang_Kai-shek.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zhang_Xueliang_and_Chiang_Kai-shek.jpg)

Zhang Xueliang with [Chiang Kai-shek](/wiki/Chiang_Kai-shek "Chiang Kai-shek") in November 1930

### Japan seizes Manchuria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interwar_period&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Japan seizes Manchuria")]

Main article: [Japanese invasion of Manchuria](/wiki/Japanese_invasion_of_Manchuria "Japanese invasion of Manchuria")

In September 1931, the Japanese [Kwantung Army](/wiki/Kwantung_Army "Kwantung Army")—acting on its own without government approval—[seized control of Manchuria](/wiki/Japanese_invasion_of_Manchuria "Japanese invasion of Manchuria"), an area in northeastern China that was controlled by the powerful warlord [Zhang Xueliang](/wiki/Zhang_Xueliang "Zhang Xueliang"). It created the puppet government of [Manchukuo](/wiki/Manchukuo "Manchukuo"). Britain and France effectively controlled the League of Nations, which issued the [Lytton Report](/wiki/Lytton_Report "Lytton Report") in 1932, saying that Japan had genuine grievances, but it acted illegally in seizing the entire province. Japan quit the League, and Britain and France took no action. US Secretary of State [Henry L. Stimson](/wiki/Henry_L._Stimson "Henry L. Stimson") announced that the United States would also not recognise Japan's conquest as legitimate. Germany welcomed Japan's actions.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### Towards the conquest of China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interwar_period&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Towards the conquest of China")]

Main article: [Second Sino-Japanese War](/wiki/Second_Sino-Japanese_War "Second Sino-Japanese War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/First_pictures_of_the_Japanese_occupation_of_Peiping_in_China.jpg/250px-First_pictures_of_the_Japanese_occupation_of_Peiping_in_China.jpg)](/wiki/File:First_pictures_of_the_Japanese_occupation_of_Peiping_in_China.jpg)

Japanese march into [Zhengyangmen](/wiki/Zhengyangmen "Zhengyangmen") of Beijing after capturing the city in July 1937

The civilian government in [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo") tried to minimise the Army's aggression in Manchuria, and announced it was withdrawing. On the contrary, the Army completed the conquest of Manchuria, and the civilian cabinet resigned. The political parties were divided on the issue of military expansion. Prime Minister Inukai tried to negotiate with China but was assassinated in the May 15 Incident in 1932, which ushered in an era of [nationalism](/wiki/Japanese_nationalism "Japanese nationalism") and [militarism](/wiki/Japanese_militarism "Japanese militarism") led by the [Imperial Japanese Army](/wiki/Imperial_Japanese_Army "Imperial Japanese Army") and supported by other right-wing societies. The IJA's nationalism ended civilian rule in Japan until after 1945.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

The Army, however, was itself divided into cliques and factions with different strategic viewpoints. One faction viewed the Soviet Union as the main enemy; the other sought to build a mighty empire based in Manchuria and northern China. The Navy, while smaller and less influential, was also factionalised. Large-scale warfare, known as the [Second Sino-Japanese War](/wiki/Second_Sino-Japanese_War "Second Sino-Japanese War"), began in August 1937, with [naval and infantry attacks focused on Shanghai](/wiki/Battle_of_Shanghai "Battle of Shanghai"), which quickly spread to other major cities. There were numerous [large-scale atrocities](/wiki/Japanese_war_crimes "Japanese war crimes") against Chinese civilians, such as the [Nanjing massacre](/wiki/Nanjing_massacre "Nanjing massacre") in December 1937, with mass murder and mass rape. By 1939 military lines had stabilised, with Japan in control of almost all of the major Chinese cities and industrial areas. A puppet government was set up.[[40]](#cite_note-FairbankReischauerCraig-40): 589–613 In the U.S., government and public opinion—even including those who were isolationist regarding Europe—was resolutely opposed to Japan and gave strong support to China. Meanwhile, the Japanese Army fared badly in large battles with the [Soviet Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") in Mongolia at the [Battles of Khalkhin Gol](/wiki/Battles_of_Khalkhin_Gol "Battles of Khalkhin Gol") in summer 1939. The USSR was too powerful. Tokyo and Moscow [signed a nonaggression treaty in April 1941](/wiki/Soviet%E2%80%93Japanese_Neutrality_Pact "Soviet–Japanese Neutrality Pact"), as the militarists turned their attention to the European colonies to the south which had urgently needed oil fields.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

## Spain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interwar_period&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Spain")]

Main articles: [Second Spanish Republic](/wiki/Second_Spanish_Republic "Second Spanish Republic") and [Francoist Spain](/wiki/Francoist_Spain "Francoist Spain")

### Spanish Civil War (1936–1939)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interwar_period&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Spanish Civil War (1936–1939)")]

Main article: [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War")

To one degree or another, Spain had been unstable politically for centuries, and in 1936–1939 was wracked by one of the bloodiest civil wars of the 20th century. The real importance comes from outside countries. In Spain the [conservative](/wiki/Conservatism "Conservatism") and [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Spain "Catholic Church in Spain") elements and the army revolted against the newly elected government of the [Second Spanish Republic](/wiki/Second_Spanish_Republic "Second Spanish Republic"), and full-scale civil war erupted. Fascist Italy and Nazi Germany gave munitions and strong military units to the rebel [Nationalist faction](/wiki/Nationalist_faction_(Spanish_Civil_War) "Nationalist faction (Spanish Civil War)"), led by General [Francisco Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco"). The [Republican (or "Loyalist") government](/wiki/Republican_faction_(Spanish_Civil_War) "Republican faction (Spanish Civil War)"), was on the defensive, but it received significant help from the Soviet Union and Mexico. Led by Great Britain and France, and including the United States, most countries remained neutral and refused to provide armaments to either side. The powerful fear was that this localised conflict would escalate into a European conflagration that no one wanted.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47)

The Spanish Civil War was marked by numerous small battles and sieges, and many atrocities, until the Nationalists won in 1939 by overwhelming the Republican forces. The Soviet Union provided armaments but never enough to equip the heterogeneous government militias and the "[International Brigades](/wiki/International_Brigades "International Brigades")" of outside [far-left](/wiki/Far-left_politics "Far-left politics") volunteers. The civil war did not escalate into a larger conflict, but did become a worldwide ideological battleground that pitted all the [Communists](/wiki/Communism "Communism") and many [socialists](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism") and [liberals](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism") against [Catholics](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), conservatives and fascists. Worldwide there was a decline in [pacifism](/wiki/Pacifism "Pacifism") and a growing sense that another [world war](/wiki/World_war "World war") was imminent, and that it would be worth fighting for.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)

## Great Britain and British Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interwar_period&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Great Britain and British Empire")]

Main articles: [Interwar Britain](/wiki/Interwar_Britain "Interwar Britain") and [War Plan Red](/wiki/War_Plan_Red "War Plan Red")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/British_Empire_1921.png/500px-British_Empire_1921.png)](/wiki/File:British_Empire_1921.png)

The Second British Empire at its territorial peak in 1921

The changing world order brought about by the war, in particular the growth of the United States and Japan as naval powers, and the rise of independence movements in India and Ireland, caused a major reassessment of British imperial policy.[[50]](#cite_note-50) Forced to choose between alignment with the United States or Japan, Britain opted not to renew the [Anglo-Japanese Alliance](/wiki/Anglo-Japanese_Alliance "Anglo-Japanese Alliance") and instead signed the 1922 [Washington Naval Treaty](/wiki/Washington_Naval_Treaty "Washington Naval Treaty"), in which Britain accepted naval parity with the United States. The issue of the empire's security was a serious concern in Britain, as it was vital to the British pride, its finance, and its trade-oriented economy.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/ImperialConference.jpg/250px-ImperialConference.jpg)](/wiki/File:ImperialConference.jpg)

[George V](/wiki/George_V "George V") with the British and Dominion prime ministers at the [1926 Imperial Conference](/wiki/1926_Imperial_Conference "1926 Imperial Conference")

[India](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") strongly supported the Empire in the First World War. It expected a reward, but failed to get [self-government](/wiki/Self-government "Self-government") as the government was still kept in control of British hands and feared another rebellion like [that of 1857](/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857"). The [Government of India Act 1919](/wiki/Government_of_India_Act_1919 "Government of India Act 1919") failed to satisfy demand for self-rule. Mounting tension, particularly in the [Punjab region](/wiki/Punjab_region "Punjab region"), culminated in the [Amritsar Massacre](/wiki/Jallianwala_Bagh_massacre "Jallianwala Bagh massacre") in 1919. [Indian nationalism](/wiki/Indian_nationalism "Indian nationalism") surged and centred in the [Congress Party](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress") led by [Mohandas Gandhi](/wiki/Mahatma_Gandhi "Mahatma Gandhi").[[53]](#cite_note-53) In Britain, public opinion was divided over the morality of the massacre between those who saw it as having saved India from anarchy and those who viewed it with revulsion.[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-Mowat_12-55)

Egypt had been under [*de facto* British control](/wiki/History_of_Egypt_under_the_British "History of Egypt under the British") since the 1880s, despite its nominal ownership by the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"). In 1922, the [Kingdom of Egypt](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Egypt "Kingdom of Egypt") was granted [formal independence](/wiki/Unilateral_Declaration_of_Egyptian_Independence "Unilateral Declaration of Egyptian Independence"), though it continued to be a [client state](/wiki/Client_state "Client state") following British guidance. Egypt joined the League of Nations. Egypt's [King Fuad](/wiki/Fuad_I_of_Egypt "Fuad I of Egypt") and his son [King Farouk](/wiki/Farouk_of_Egypt "Farouk of Egypt") and their conservative allies stayed in power with lavish lifestyles thanks to an informal alliance with Britain who would protect them from both secular and Muslim radicalism.[[56]](#cite_note-56) [Mandatory Iraq](/wiki/Mandatory_Iraq "Mandatory Iraq"), a British [mandate](/wiki/League_of_Nations_mandate "League of Nations mandate") since 1920, gained official independence as the [Kingdom of Iraq](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Iraq "Kingdom of Iraq") in 1932 when [King Faisal](/wiki/Faisal_I_of_Iraq "Faisal I of Iraq") agreed to British terms of a military alliance and an assured flow of oil.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)

In [Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine"), Britain was presented with the problem of mediating between the [Palestinian Arabs](/wiki/Palestinians "Palestinians") and increasing numbers of [Jewish settlers](/wiki/Yishuv "Yishuv"). The [Balfour Declaration](/wiki/Balfour_Declaration "Balfour Declaration"), which had been incorporated into the terms of the mandate, said that a national home for the [Jewish people](/wiki/Jews "Jews") would be established in Palestine, and Jewish immigration allowed up to a limit that would be determined by the mandatory power. This led to increasing conflict with the Arab population, who openly [revolted in 1936](/wiki/1936%E2%80%9339_Arab_revolt_in_Palestine "1936–39 Arab revolt in Palestine"). As the threat of war with Germany increased during the 1930s, Britain judged the support of Arabs as more important than the establishment of a Jewish homeland, and shifted to a pro-Arab stance, limiting Jewish immigration and in turn triggering a [Jewish insurgency](/wiki/Jewish_insurgency_in_Palestine "Jewish insurgency in Palestine").[[55]](#cite_note-Mowat_12-55): 269–96

The Dominions (Canada, Newfoundland, Australia, New Zealand, South Africa and the Irish Free State) were self-governing and gained semi-independence in the World War, while Britain still controlled foreign policy and defence in all except Ireland. The right of the Dominions to set their own foreign policy was recognised in 1923 and formalised by the 1931 [Statute of Westminster](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931 "Statute of Westminster 1931"). The Irish Free State effectively broke all ties with Britain in 1937, leaving the Commonwealth and becoming an [independent republic](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland").[[55]](#cite_note-Mowat_12-55): 373–402

## French Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interwar_period&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: French Empire")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/FrenchEmpire1946.png/500px-FrenchEmpire1946.png)](/wiki/File:FrenchEmpire1946.png)

The French Empire from 1919 to 1949

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Paris_expo_1937.jpg/250px-Paris_expo_1937.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paris_expo_1937.jpg)

Place de Varsovie in [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") during the [World Expo](/wiki/Exposition_Internationale_des_Arts_et_Techniques_dans_la_Vie_Moderne "Exposition Internationale des Arts et Techniques dans la Vie Moderne") in 1937 ([Agfacolor](/wiki/Agfacolor "Agfacolor") photo)

Main articles: [Interwar France](/wiki/Interwar_France "Interwar France") and [French colonial empire](/wiki/French_colonial_empire "French colonial empire")

French census statistics from 1938 show an imperial population with France at over 150 million people, outside of France itself, of 102.8 million people living on 13.5 million square kilometers. Of the total population, 64.7 million lived in Africa and 31.2 million lived in Asia; 900,000 lived in the [French West Indies](/wiki/French_West_Indies "French West Indies") or islands in the South Pacific. The largest colonies were [French Indochina](/wiki/French_Indochina "French Indochina") with 26.8 million (in five separate colonies), [French Algeria](/wiki/French_Algeria "French Algeria") with 6.6 million, the [French protectorate in Morocco](/wiki/French_protectorate_in_Morocco "French protectorate in Morocco"), with 5.4 million, and [French West Africa](/wiki/French_West_Africa "French West Africa") with 35.2 million in nine colonies. The total includes 1.9 million Europeans, and 350,000 "assimilated" natives.[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)

### Revolt in North Africa against Spain and France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interwar_period&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Revolt in North Africa against Spain and France")]

Main article: [Rif War](/wiki/Rif_War "Rif War")

The Berber independence leader [Abd el-Krim](/wiki/Abd_el-Krim "Abd el-Krim") (1882–1963) organised armed resistance against the Spanish and French for control of Morocco. The Spanish had faced unrest off and on from the 1890s, but in 1921, Spanish forces were massacred at the [Battle of Annual](/wiki/Battle_of_Annual "Battle of Annual"). El-Krim founded an independent [Rif Republic](/wiki/Rif_Republic "Rif Republic"), which operated until 1926, but had no international recognition. Eventually, France and Spain agreed to end the revolt. They sent in 200,000 soldiers, forcing el-Krim to surrender in 1926; he was exiled in the Pacific until 1947. Morocco was now [pacified](/wiki/Pacification_(military_action) "Pacification (military action)"), and became the base from which [Spanish Nationalists](/wiki/Nationalist_faction_(Spanish_Civil_War) "Nationalist faction (Spanish Civil War)") would launch their [rebellion](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War") against the [Spanish Republic](/wiki/Second_Spanish_Republic "Second Spanish Republic") in 1936.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

## Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interwar_period&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Germany")]

Main article: [History of Germany](/wiki/History_of_Germany "History of Germany")

### Weimar Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interwar_period&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Weimar Republic")]

Main article: [Weimar Republic](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-K0623-0502-001%2C_Berlin%2C_Tanztee_im_%22Esplanade%22.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-K0623-0502-001%2C_Berlin%2C_Tanztee_im_%22Esplanade%22.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-K0623-0502-001,_Berlin,_Tanztee_im_%22Esplanade%22.jpg)

The "[Golden Twenties](/wiki/Golden_Twenties "Golden Twenties")" in Berlin: a jazz band plays for a tea dance at the hotel Esplanade, 1926

The peace terms in the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles") provoked bitter indignation throughout Germany, and seriously weakened the new democratic regime. The Treaty stripped Germany of all of its [overseas colonies](/wiki/German_colonial_empire "German colonial empire"), of [Alsace–Lorraine](/wiki/Alsace%E2%80%93Lorraine "Alsace–Lorraine"), and of predominantly Polish districts. The Allied armies occupied industrial sectors in western Germany including the Rhineland, and Germany was not allowed to have a real army, navy, or air force. [Reparations](/wiki/World_War_I_reparations "World War I reparations") were demanded, especially by France, involving shipments of raw materials, as well as annual payments.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

When Germany defaulted on its reparation payments, [French and Belgian troops occupied the heavily industrialised Ruhr district](/wiki/Occupation_of_the_Ruhr "Occupation of the Ruhr") (January 1923). The German government encouraged the population of the Ruhr to [passive resistance](/wiki/Nonviolent_resistance "Nonviolent resistance"): shops would not sell goods to the foreign soldiers, coal mines would not dig for the foreign troops, trams in which members of the occupation army had taken seat would be left abandoned in the middle of the street. Rather than raise taxes, the German government printed vast quantities of paper money, much of which was spent to pay striking workers in the Ruhr and to subsidise inactive factories and mines, causing [hyperinflation](/wiki/Hyperinflation_in_the_Weimar_Republic "Hyperinflation in the Weimar Republic"), which also damaged the [French economy](/wiki/Economy_of_France "Economy of France"). The passive resistance proved effective, insofar as the occupation became a loss-making deal for the French government. But the hyperinflation caused many prudent savers to lose all the money they had saved. Weimar added new internal enemies every year, as anti-democratic [Nazis](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party"), [Nationalists](/wiki/German_nationalism "German nationalism"), and [Communists](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Germany "Communist Party of Germany") battled each other in the streets.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

Germany was the first state to establish diplomatic relations with the new [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"). Under the [Treaty of Rapallo](/wiki/Treaty_of_Rapallo,_1922 "Treaty of Rapallo, 1922"), Germany accorded the Soviet Union *de jure* recognition, and the two signatories mutually agreed to cancel all pre-war debts and renounced war claims. In October 1925 the [Treaty of Locarno](/wiki/Treaty_of_Locarno "Treaty of Locarno") was signed by Germany, France, Belgium, Britain, and Italy; it recognised Germany's borders with France and Belgium. Moreover, Britain, Italy, and Belgium undertook to assist France in the case that German troops marched into the demilitarised Rhineland. Locarno paved the way for Germany's admission to the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") in 1926.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

### Nazi era, 1933–1939

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interwar_period&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Nazi era, 1933–1939")]

Main articles: [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and [Causes of World War II](/wiki/Causes_of_World_War_II "Causes of World War II")

Further information: [German rearmament](/wiki/German_rearmament "German rearmament")

Hitler came to power in January 1933, and inaugurated an aggressive power designed to give Germany economic and political domination across central Europe. He did not attempt to recover the lost colonies. Until August 1939, the Nazis denounced Communists and the Soviet Union as the greatest enemy, along with the Jews.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/1938_Naka_yoshi_sangoku.jpg/250px-1938_Naka_yoshi_sangoku.jpg)](/wiki/File:1938_Naka_yoshi_sangoku.jpg)

A Japanese poster promoting the [Axis cooperation](/wiki/Axis_Powers "Axis Powers") in 1938

Hitler's diplomatic strategy in the 1930s was to make seemingly reasonable demands, threatening war if they were not met. When opponents tried to appease him, he accepted the gains that were offered, then went to the next target. That aggressive strategy worked as Germany pulled out of the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations"), rejected the [Versailles Treaty](/wiki/Versailles_Treaty "Versailles Treaty"), and began to rearm. Retaking the [Territory of the Saar Basin](/wiki/Territory_of_the_Saar_Basin "Territory of the Saar Basin") in the aftermath of a [plebiscite](/wiki/1935_Saar_status_referendum "1935 Saar status referendum") that favoured returning to Germany, [Hitler's Germany remilitarised the Rhineland](/wiki/Remilitarization_of_the_Rhineland "Remilitarization of the Rhineland"), formed the [Pact of Steel](/wiki/Pact_of_Steel "Pact of Steel") alliance with Mussolini's Italy, and sent massive military aid to Franco in the Spanish Civil War. [Germany seized Austria](/wiki/Anschluss "Anschluss"), considered to be a German state, in 1938, and [took over Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Occupation_of_Czechoslovakia_(1938%E2%80%931945) "Occupation of Czechoslovakia (1938–1945)") after the [Munich Agreement](/wiki/Munich_Agreement "Munich Agreement") with Britain and France. Forming a [non-aggression pact](/wiki/Molotov%E2%80%93Ribbentrop_Pact "Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact") with the Soviet Union in August 1939, [Germany invaded Poland](/wiki/Invasion_of_Poland "Invasion of Poland") after Poland's refusal to cede the [Free City of Danzig](/wiki/Free_City_of_Danzig "Free City of Danzig") in September 1939. Britain and France declared war and [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") began – somewhat sooner than the Nazis expected or were ready for.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Polish_Army_Warsaw_1939.jpg/250px-Polish_Army_Warsaw_1939.jpg)](/wiki/File:Polish_Army_Warsaw_1939.jpg)

A Polish Army soldier holds the last remaining part of a German [Heinkel He 111](/wiki/Heinkel_He_111 "Heinkel He 111") bomber shot down by Poles over Warsaw when an airplane was [killing civilians](/wiki/Invasion_of_Poland "Invasion of Poland") in September 1939 ([Kodachrome](/wiki/Kodachrome "Kodachrome") photo).

After establishing the "[Rome-Berlin Axis](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers")" with [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini"), and signing the [Anti-Comintern Pact](/wiki/Anti-Comintern_Pact "Anti-Comintern Pact") with Japan – which was joined by Italy a year later in 1937 – Hitler felt able to take the offensive in foreign policy. On 12 March 1938, German troops marched into Austria, where an [attempted Nazi coup](/wiki/July_Putsch "July Putsch") had been unsuccessful in 1934. When Austrian-born Hitler entered [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna"), he was greeted by loud cheers. Four weeks later, 99% of Austrians voted in favour of the annexation ([Anschluss](/wiki/Anschluss "Anschluss")) of their country Austria to the [German Reich](/wiki/German_Reich_(1933-1945) "German Reich (1933-1945)"). After Austria, Hitler turned to [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia"), where the 3.5 million-strong [Sudeten German](/wiki/Sudeten_Germans "Sudeten Germans") minority was demanding equal rights and self-government.[[68]](#cite_note-Donald_Cameron_Watt_1989-68)[[69]](#cite_note-R.J._Overy,_2014-69)

At the [Munich Conference](/wiki/Munich_Agreement "Munich Agreement") of September 1938, Hitler, the Italian leader Benito Mussolini, British Prime Minister [Neville Chamberlain](/wiki/Neville_Chamberlain "Neville Chamberlain"), and French Prime Minister [Édouard Daladier](/wiki/%C3%89douard_Daladier "Édouard Daladier") agreed upon the cession of [Sudeten](/wiki/Sudetenland "Sudetenland") territory to the German Reich by [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia"). Hitler then declared that all of German Reich's territorial claims had been fulfilled. However, hardly six months after the Munich Agreement, in March 1939, Hitler used the smouldering quarrel between [Slovaks](/wiki/Slovak_people "Slovak people") and [Czechs](/wiki/Czechs "Czechs") as a pretext for taking over the rest of Czechoslovakia as the [Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia](/wiki/Protectorate_of_Bohemia_and_Moravia "Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia"). In the same month, he secured the return of [Memel](/wiki/Klaip%C4%97da_Region "Klaipėda Region") from [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania") to Germany. Chamberlain was forced to acknowledge that his policy of [appeasement](/wiki/Appeasement "Appeasement") towards Hitler had failed.[[68]](#cite_note-Donald_Cameron_Watt_1989-68)[[69]](#cite_note-R.J._Overy,_2014-69)

## Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interwar_period&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Italy")]

Main article: [Fascist Italy](/wiki/Fascist_Italy "Fascist Italy")

Further information: [Second Italo-Ethiopian War](/wiki/Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War "Second Italo-Ethiopian War"), [Italian invasion of Albania](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_Albania "Italian invasion of Albania"), and [History of Greece (1924–1941)](/wiki/History_of_Greece_(1924%E2%80%931941) "History of Greece (1924–1941)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Italy_aims_Europe_1936.png/250px-Italy_aims_Europe_1936.png)](/wiki/File:Italy_aims_Europe_1936.png)

Legend:

[Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania"), which was a client state, was considered a territory to be annexed.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Impero_italiano.svg/250px-Impero_italiano.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Impero_italiano.svg)

Maximum extent of imperial Italy

Pre-Second World War

Captured during the Second World War

In 1922, the leader of the [Italian Fascist](/wiki/Italian_Fascism "Italian Fascism") movement, [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini"), was appointed [Prime Minister of Italy](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Italy "Prime Minister of Italy") after the [March on Rome](/wiki/March_on_Rome "March on Rome"). Mussolini resolved the question of sovereignty over the [Dodecanese](/wiki/Dodecanese "Dodecanese") at the 1923 [Treaty of Lausanne](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lausanne "Treaty of Lausanne"), which formalised Italian administration of both [Libya](/wiki/Italian_Libya "Italian Libya") and the [Dodecanese Islands](/wiki/Italian_Islands_of_the_Aegean "Italian Islands of the Aegean"), in return for a payment to [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"), the successor state to the Ottoman Empire, though he failed in an attempt to extract a mandate of a portion of Iraq from Britain.

During the month after the ratification of the Treaty of Lausanne, Mussolini ordered the invasion of the Greek island of [Corfu](/wiki/Corfu "Corfu") after the [Corfu incident](/wiki/Corfu_incident "Corfu incident"). The [Italian press](/wiki/Media_of_Italy "Media of Italy") supported the move, noting that Corfu had been a [Venetian possession](/wiki/Venetian_Ionian_Islands "Venetian Ionian Islands") for four hundred years. The matter was taken by [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") to the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations"), where Mussolini was convinced by Britain to evacuate [Royal Italian Army](/wiki/Royal_Italian_Army "Royal Italian Army") troops, in return for reparations from Greece. The confrontation led Britain and Italy to resolve the question of [Jubaland](/wiki/Jubaland "Jubaland") in 1924, which was merged into [Italian Somaliland](/wiki/Italian_Somaliland "Italian Somaliland").[[70]](#cite_note-70)

During the late 1920s, imperial expansion became an increasingly favoured theme in Mussolini's speeches.[[71]](#cite_note-smith-71) Amongst Mussolini's aims were that Italy had to become the dominant power in the Mediterranean which would be able to challenge France or Britain, as well as attain access to the [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") and [Indian Oceans](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean").[[71]](#cite_note-smith-71) Mussolini alleged that Italy required uncontested access to the world's oceans and shipping lanes to ensure its national sovereignty.[[72]](#cite_note-salerno-72) He elaborated on this in a document he later drew up in 1939 called "The March to the Oceans", which was included in the official records of a meeting of the [Grand Council of Fascism](/wiki/Grand_Council_of_Fascism "Grand Council of Fascism").[[72]](#cite_note-salerno-72) The text asserted that maritime position determined a nation's independence: countries with free access to the high seas were independent; while those who lacked this, were not. Italy, which only had access to an inland sea without French and British acquiescence, was only a "semi-independent nation", and alleged to be a "prisoner in the Mediterranean":[[72]](#cite_note-salerno-72)

> The bars of this prison are [Corsica](/wiki/Corsica "Corsica"), [Tunisia](/wiki/French_protectorate_of_Tunisia "French protectorate of Tunisia"), [Malta](/wiki/Crown_Colony_of_Malta "Crown Colony of Malta"), and [Cyprus](/wiki/British_Cyprus "British Cyprus"). The guards of this prison are [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar") and [Suez](/wiki/Suez_Canal "Suez Canal"). Corsica is a pistol pointed at the heart of Italy; Tunisia at Sicily. Malta and Cyprus constitute a threat to all our positions in the eastern and western Mediterranean. Greece, Turkey, and [Egypt](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Egypt "Kingdom of Egypt") have been ready to form a chain with Great Britain and to complete the politico-military encirclement of Italy. Thus Greece, Turkey, and Egypt must be considered vital enemies of Italy's expansion... The aim of Italian policy, which cannot have, and does not have continental objectives of a European territorial nature except Albania, is first of all to break the bars of this prison... Once the bars are broken, Italian policy can only have one motto—to march to the oceans.

— Benito Mussolini, The March to the Oceans[[72]](#cite_note-salerno-72)

In the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans"), the Fascist regime claimed [Dalmatia](/wiki/Dalmatia "Dalmatia") and held ambitions over [Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania"), [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia"), [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia"), [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina"), [Macedonia](/wiki/Macedonia_(region) "Macedonia (region)"), and [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") based on the precedent of previous Roman dominance in these regions.[[73]](#cite_note-Robert_Bideleux_1998._Pp._467-73) Dalmatia and Slovenia were to be directly annexed into Italy while the remainder of the Balkans was to be transformed into Italian client states.[[74]](#cite_note-74) The regime also sought to establish protective patron-client relationships with [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary"), [Romania](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Romania "Kingdom of Romania"), and [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria").[[73]](#cite_note-Robert_Bideleux_1998._Pp._467-73)

In both 1932 and 1935, Italy demanded a [League of Nations mandate](/wiki/League_of_Nations_mandate "League of Nations mandate") of the former [German Cameroon](/wiki/German_colonial_empire "German colonial empire") and a free hand in the [Ethiopian Empire](/wiki/Ethiopian_Empire "Ethiopian Empire") from France in return for Italian support against Germany in the [Stresa Front](/wiki/Stresa_Front "Stresa Front").[[75]](#cite_note-burgwyn-75) That was refused by French Prime Minister [Édouard Herriot](/wiki/%C3%89douard_Herriot "Édouard Herriot"), who was not yet sufficiently worried about the prospect of a German resurgence.[[75]](#cite_note-burgwyn-75) The failed resolution of the [Abyssinia Crisis](/wiki/Abyssinia_Crisis "Abyssinia Crisis") led to the [Second Italo-Ethiopian War](/wiki/Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War "Second Italo-Ethiopian War"), in which Italy annexed Ethiopia to its empire.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Italy's stance towards Spain shifted between the 1920s and the 1930s. The Fascist regime in the 1920s held deep antagonism towards Spain due to [Miguel Primo de Rivera](/wiki/Miguel_Primo_de_Rivera "Miguel Primo de Rivera")'s pro-French foreign policy. In 1926, Mussolini began aiding the [Catalan separatist movement](/wiki/Catalan_separatism "Catalan separatism"), which was led by [Francesc Macià](/wiki/Francesc_Maci%C3%A0 "Francesc Macià"), against the Spanish government.[[76]](#cite_note-Robert_H._Whealey_2005._P._11-76) With the rise of the left-wing Republican government replacing the [Spanish monarchy](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Spain "Monarchy of Spain"), Spanish monarchists and fascists repeatedly approached Italy for aid in overthrowing the Republican government, in which Italy agreed to support them to establish a pro-Italian government in Spain.[[76]](#cite_note-Robert_H._Whealey_2005._P._11-76) In July 1936, [Francisco Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco") of the Nationalist faction in the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War") requested Italian support against the ruling Republican faction, and guaranteed that, if Italy supported the Nationalists, "future relations would be more than friendly" and that Italian support "would have permitted the influence of Rome to prevail over that of Berlin in the future politics of Spain".[[77]](#cite_note-77) Italy intervened in the civil war with the intention of occupying the [Balearic Islands](/wiki/Balearic_Islands "Balearic Islands") and creating a [client state](/wiki/Client_state "Client state") in Spain.[[78]](#cite_note-78) Italy sought the control of the Balearic Islands due to its strategic position—Italy could use the islands as a base to disrupt the lines of communication between France and its [North African colonies](/wiki/French_North_Africa "French North Africa") and between [British Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar") and [Malta](/wiki/Crown_Colony_of_Malta "Crown Colony of Malta").[[79]](#cite_note-79) After the victory by Franco and the Nationalists in the war, Allied intelligence was informed that Italy was pressuring Spain to permit an Italian occupation of the [Balearic Islands](/wiki/Balearic_Islands "Balearic Islands").[[80]](#cite_note-80)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Unione_tunisi_31octobre1938.jpg/250px-Unione_tunisi_31octobre1938.jpg)](/wiki/File:Unione_tunisi_31octobre1938.jpg)

Italian newspaper in Tunisia that represented Italians living in the [French protectorate of Tunisia](/wiki/French_protectorate_of_Tunisia "French protectorate of Tunisia").

After Great Britain signed the Anglo-Italian [Easter Accords](/wiki/Easter_Accords "Easter Accords") in 1938, Mussolini and Foreign Minister [Galeazzo Ciano](/wiki/Galeazzo_Ciano "Galeazzo Ciano") issued demands for concessions in the Mediterranean by France, particularly regarding [French Somaliland](/wiki/French_Somaliland "French Somaliland"), [Tunisia](/wiki/French_protectorate_of_Tunisia "French protectorate of Tunisia") and the French-run [Suez Canal](/wiki/Suez_Canal "Suez Canal").[[81]](#cite_note-Reynolds_Mathewson_Salerno_1940._p82-83-81) Three weeks later, Mussolini told Ciano that he intended for an Italian takeover of Albania.[[81]](#cite_note-Reynolds_Mathewson_Salerno_1940._p82-83-81) Mussolini professed that Italy would only be able to "breathe easily" if it had acquired a contiguous colonial domain in Africa from the Atlantic to the Indian Oceans, and when ten million Italians had settled in them.[[71]](#cite_note-smith-71) In 1938, Italy demanded a [sphere of influence](/wiki/Sphere_of_influence "Sphere of influence") in the Suez Canal in [Egypt](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Egypt "Kingdom of Egypt"), specifically demanding that the French-dominated [Suez Canal Company](/wiki/Suez_Canal_Company "Suez Canal Company") accept an Italian representative on its board of directors.[[82]](#cite_note-LIFE_1938._Pp._23-82) Italy opposed the French monopoly over the [Suez Canal](/wiki/Suez_Canal "Suez Canal") because, under the French-dominated Suez Canal Company, all merchant traffic to the [Italian East Africa](/wiki/Italian_East_Africa "Italian East Africa") colony was forced to pay tolls on entering the canal.[[82]](#cite_note-LIFE_1938._Pp._23-82)

Albanian Prime Minister and President [Ahmet Zogu](/wiki/Zog_I_of_Albania "Zog I of Albania"), who had, in 1928, proclaimed himself [King of Albania](/wiki/King_of_Albania "King of Albania"), failed to create a stable state.[[83]](#cite_note-83) Albanian society was deeply divided by religion and language, with a border dispute with Greece and an undeveloped, rural economy. In 1939, Italy [invaded and annexed Albania](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_Albania "Italian invasion of Albania") as a separate kingdom in [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") with the Italian crown. Italy had long built strong links with the Albanian leadership and considered it firmly within its sphere of influence. Mussolini wanted a spectacular success over a smaller neighbour to match Germany's [annexation of Austria](/wiki/Anschluss "Anschluss") and [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Occupation_of_Czechoslovakia "Occupation of Czechoslovakia"). Italian King [Victor Emmanuel III](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_III_of_Italy "Victor Emmanuel III of Italy") took the [Albanian crown](/wiki/King_of_Albania "King of Albania"), and a [fascist government](/wiki/Italian_protectorate_of_Albania_(1939%E2%80%931943) "Italian protectorate of Albania (1939–1943)") under [Shefqet Vërlaci](/wiki/Shefqet_V%C3%ABrlaci "Shefqet Vërlaci") was established.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

## Soviet Union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interwar_period&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Soviet Union")]

The newly formed Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic declared [war on Poland](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Soviet_War "Polish–Soviet War") in 1919. This war lasted until 1921 and resulted in a Polish victory.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

The Soviet Union Would be formed in 1922, led by Vladimir Lenin. In 1924, Vladimir Lenin's death led to a power struggle between various members of the Communist Party.[[86]](#cite_note-86) It ended with [Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin") becoming the ruler of the USSR. Once in power, Stalin initiated the first Five Year Plan, which involved [mass seizure of property from private citizens](/wiki/Dekulakization "Dekulakization").[[87]](#cite_note-87) During this process, over 2 million people were relocated to "special settlements" and 20,000 to 30,000 were executed.

In the early 1930s, the area that now constitutes Ukraine experienced a massive famine known as the [Holodomor](/wiki/Holodomor "Holodomor").[[88]](#cite_note-88) The Holodomor's death toll is impossible to calculate precisely but believed to be anywhere from 2 to 5 million people. It is unclear whether the famine was due to unintentional mismanagement or an attempt at genocide via starvation.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

## Regional patterns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interwar_period&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Regional patterns")]

### Balkans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interwar_period&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Balkans")]

The [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression_in_Romania "Great Depression in Romania") destabilised the [Kingdom of Romania](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Romania "Kingdom of Romania"). The early 1930s were marked by social unrest, high unemployment, and strikes. In several instances, the Romanian government violently repressed strikes and riots, notably the 1929 miners' strike in [Valea Jiului](/wiki/Valea_Jiului "Valea Jiului") and the strike in the [Grivița](/wiki/Grivi%C8%9Ba "Grivița") railroad workshops. In the mid-1930s, the [Romanian economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Romania "Economy of Romania") recovered and the industry grew significantly, although about 80% of Romanians were still employed in [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Romania "Agriculture in Romania"). French economic and political influence was predominant in the early 1920s but then Germany became more dominant, especially in the 1930s.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

In the [Albanian Kingdom](/wiki/Albanian_Kingdom_(1928%E2%80%931939) "Albanian Kingdom (1928–1939)"), [Zog I](/wiki/Zog_I_of_Albania "Zog I of Albania") introduced new civil codes, constitutional changes and attempted [land reforms](/wiki/Land_reform_in_Albania "Land reform in Albania"), the latter which was largely unsuccessful due to the inadequacy of the [country's banking system](/wiki/Banking_in_Albania "Banking in Albania"), which could not deal with advanced reformist transactions. Albania's reliance on Italy also grew as Italians exercised control over nearly every Albanian official through money and patronage, breeding a colonial-like mentality.[[91]](#cite_note-Gerwarth-91)

Ethnic integration and assimilation was a major problem faced by the newly formed post-World War I Balkan states, which were compounded by historical differences. In the [Kingdom of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia") for instance, its most influential element was the pre-war [Kingdom of Serbia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Serbia "Kingdom of Serbia") but also integrated states like Slovenia and Croatia, which were part of [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary"). With new territories came varying legal systems, social structures and political structures. Social and economic development rates also varied as for example Slovenia and Croatia was far more advanced economically than Kosovo and Macedonia.
