### Latin America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interwar_period&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Latin America")]

The United States launched [minor interventions](/wiki/Banana_Wars "Banana Wars") into Latin America including a military presence in [Cuba](/wiki/Republic_of_Cuba_(1902%E2%80%931959) "Republic of Cuba (1902–1959)"), Panama with the [Panama Canal Zone](/wiki/Panama_Canal_Zone "Panama Canal Zone"), [Haiti](/wiki/United_States_occupation_of_Haiti "United States occupation of Haiti") (1915–1935), [Dominican Republic](/wiki/United_States_occupation_of_the_Dominican_Republic_(1916%E2%80%931924) "United States occupation of the Dominican Republic (1916–1924)") (1916–1924), and [Nicaragua](/wiki/United_States_occupation_of_Nicaragua "United States occupation of Nicaragua") (1912–1933). The [U.S. Marine Corps](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps") began to specialise in long-term military occupation of these countries.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

The [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression_in_Latin_America "Great Depression in Latin America") posed a great challenge to the region. The collapse of the world economy meant that the demand for raw materials drastically declined, undermining many of the economies of Latin America. Intellectuals and government leaders in Latin America turned their backs on the older economic policies and turned toward [import substitution industrialisation](/wiki/Import_substitution_industrialisation "Import substitution industrialisation"). The goal was to create self-sufficient economies, which would have their own industrial sectors and large middle classes and which would be immune to the fluctuations of the global economy. Despite the potential threats to United States commercial interests, the [Roosevelt administration](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") (1933–1945) understood that the United States could not wholly oppose import substitution. Roosevelt implemented a [Good Neighbor policy](/wiki/Good_Neighbor_policy "Good Neighbor policy") and allowed the nationalisation of some American companies in Latin America. Mexican President [Lázaro Cárdenas](/wiki/L%C3%A1zaro_C%C3%A1rdenas "Lázaro Cárdenas") [nationalised American oil companies](/wiki/Mexican_oil_expropriation "Mexican oil expropriation"), from which he created [Pemex](/wiki/Pemex "Pemex"). Cárdenas presided over the [redistribution of a quantity of land](/wiki/Land_reform_in_Mexico "Land reform in Mexico"), fulfilling the hopes of many since the start of the [Mexican Revolution](/wiki/Mexican_Revolution "Mexican Revolution"). The [Platt Amendment](/wiki/Platt_Amendment "Platt Amendment") was also repealed, freeing Cuba from legal and official interference of the United States in its politics. The Second World War brought the United States and most Latin American nations together, with Argentina the main hold out.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

During the interwar period, United States policy makers continued to be concerned over German influence in Latin America.[[94]](#cite_note-Goebels-94)[[95]](#cite_note-Penny2017-95) Some analysts grossly exaggerated the influence of Germans in South America even after the First World War when German influence somewhat declined.[[95]](#cite_note-Penny2017-95)[[96]](#cite_note-Carlos2011-96) As the influence of United States grew all-over the Americas Germany concentrated its foreign policy efforts in the [Southern Cone](/wiki/Southern_Cone "Southern Cone") countries where US influence was weaker and larger German communities were at place.[[94]](#cite_note-Goebels-94)

The contrary ideals of *[indigenismo](/wiki/Indigenismo "Indigenismo")* and *[hispanismo](/wiki/Hispanismo "Hispanismo")* held sway among intellectuals in [Spanish-speaking America](/wiki/Hispanic_America "Hispanic America") during the interwar period. In Argentina the *[gaucho](/wiki/Gaucho "Gaucho")* genre flourished. A rejection of "Western universalist" influences was in vogue across Latin America.[[94]](#cite_note-Goebels-94) This last tendency was in part inspired by the translation into Spanish of the book *[Decline of the West](/wiki/Decline_of_the_West "Decline of the West")* in 1923.[[94]](#cite_note-Goebels-94)

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interwar_period&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Sports")]

Sports became increasingly popular, drawing enthusiastic fans to large stadiums.[[97]](#cite_note-97) The [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") (IOC) worked to encourage Olympic ideals and participation. After the 1922 Latin American Games in Rio de Janeiro, the IOC helped to establish national Olympic committees and prepare for future competition. In Brazil, however, sporting and political rivalries slowed progress as opposing factions fought for control of [international sport](/wiki/International_sport "International sport"). The [1924 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1924_Summer_Olympics "1924 Summer Olympics") in Paris and the [1928 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1928_Summer_Olympics "1928 Summer Olympics") in Amsterdam had greatly increased participation from Latin American athletes.[[98]](#cite_note-98)

English and Scottish engineers had brought futebol (soccer) to Brazil in the late 19th century. The International Committee of the YMCA of North America and the Playground Association of America played major roles in training coaches.[[99]](#cite_note-99) Across the globe after 1912, the [Fédération Internationale de Football Association](/wiki/F%C3%A9d%C3%A9ration_Internationale_de_Football_Association "Fédération Internationale de Football Association") (FIFA) played the chief role in the transformation of association football into a global game, working with national and regional organisations, and setting up the rules and customs, and establishing championships such as the World Cup.[[100]](#cite_note-100)
